ARINC 429 Basics.clue: This is the bus architecture used by ARINC 429 — one transmitter broadcasting to multiple receivers on a dedicated wire pair.
choices: Multi-drop bidirectional
Token-passing ring
Point-to-multipoint
Peer-to-peer full-duplex
ARINC 429 Basics.explanation: ARINC 429 uses a unidirectional, point-to-multipoint broadcast architecture: one transmitter, up to 20 receivers, no multi-drop with multiple talkers. Multi-drop bidirectional describes 1553 and RS-485. Token-passing describes token-ring networks, not ARINC 429.

ARINC 429 Basics.clue: This is the maximum data rate defined by the ARINC 429 standard.
choices: 12 kbps
1 Mbps
10 Mbps
100 kbps
ARINC 429 Basics.explanation: ARINC 429's high-speed rate is 100 kilobits per second — the maximum defined by the standard. 12 kbps is the low-speed rate. 1 Mbps is the data rate of MIL-STD-1553. 10 Mbps is well above any ARINC 429 rate.

ARINC 429 Basics.clue: This is the primary purpose of the ARINC 429 databus in modern avionics.
choices: To transfer standardized digital data between avionics units such as air data
To provide primary flight control inputs to the autopilot servos
To supply electrical power to remote sensors
To connect the engine FADEC to fuel metering valves
ARINC 429 Basics.explanation: ARINC 429 is a data-transfer standard for avionics inter-unit communication — sharing values such as altitude, airspeed, position, and status between boxes. It does not carry power, flight control commands, or engine control signals.

ARINC 429 Basics.clue: This is the most widely deployed digital databus standard across commercial and general-aviation avionics.
choices: MIL-STD-1553
CAN bus
IEEE 1394
ARINC 429
ARINC 429 Basics.explanation: ARINC 429 is the dominant digital databus in civilian aviation. MIL-STD-1553 is widely used in military applications. CAN bus is common in automotive and some UAV systems. IEEE 1394 (FireWire) is seldom used in aviation.

ARINC 429 Basics.clue: This 8-bit field at the start of every ARINC 429 word tells a receiver which parameter — altitude, airspeed, heading, etc. — the word contains.
choices: Label
Preamble
Destination tag
Parity block
ARINC 429 Basics.explanation: The Label is the first 8 bits of every 32-bit ARINC 429 word. It is octal-coded (for example, label 203 = altitude, 206 = computed airspeed). Receivers filter the transmitter's stream by Label and accept only the parameters they need. ARINC 429 does not use a separate preamble or destination tag, and parity is a single bit at the end of the word, not a block.

MIL-STD-1553.clue: This is the protocol model used by MIL-STD-1553, in which a single controller initiates every transaction.
choices: Command/response with a bus controller
Peer-to-peer with collision detection
Token-passing ring
Broadcast-only with no acknowledgements
MIL-STD-1553.explanation: MIL-STD-1553 uses a command/response model with a single bus controller directing all traffic. Peer-to-peer with collision detection describes Ethernet. Token ring is a different LAN architecture. Broadcast-only with no acknowledgements better describes ARINC 429.

MIL-STD-1553.clue: This is the function of the bus controller in a MIL-STD-1553 system.
choices: To initiate all bus transactions, schedule messages, and direct data
To passively monitor bus traffic without issuing commands
To provide power to the remote terminals
To convert analog signals from sensors into digital words
MIL-STD-1553.explanation: The bus controller initiates every transaction on the bus — issuing commands, scheduling messages, and routing data. Passive monitoring describes a bus monitor (BM), a separate role. The BC does not supply power or perform A/D conversion.

MIL-STD-1553.clue: When a replacement RT is not detected on a MIL-STD-1553 bus, this is the first item to verify.
choices: The aircraft weather radar settings
The RT's address strapping and coupler/termination wiring
The flight control computer's firmware version
The aircraft's GPS almanac
MIL-STD-1553.explanation: RT address strapping and physical bus connection (coupler, termination, stub length) are the top causes of a 1553 device not being detected. Weather radar settings, FCC firmware, and GPS almanac are unrelated.

MIL-STD-1553.clue: This is the main architectural difference between ARINC 429 and MIL-STD-1553.
choices: ARINC 429 is faster than MIL-STD-1553
ARINC 429 is unidirectional broadcast with no controller
ARINC 429 is used only in helicopters; MIL-STD-1553 is used only in jets
ARINC 429 uses optical fiber; MIL-STD-1553 uses copper
MIL-STD-1553.explanation: The architectural distinction is topology and protocol: 429 is unidirectional broadcast (no controller), 1553 is bidirectional multi-drop with a dedicated bus controller. MIL-STD-1553 is faster (1 Mbps vs 100 kbps), not slower. Both standards use copper twisted-shielded pairs in normal installations.

MIL-STD-1553.clue: This is the primary reason MIL-STD-1553 wires every Remote Terminal to two parallel cables (Bus A and Bus B).
choices: To double the data rate for high-bandwidth applications
Redundancy — traffic continues on the other bus if one fails
To separate command traffic from response traffic
To let two Bus Controllers share the bus workload
MIL-STD-1553.explanation: MIL-STD-1553 was designed for flight-critical military applications where a single wire fault must not disable the system. The two buses carry identical traffic; the Bus Controller normally uses Bus A, and if it sees errors it retries on Bus B. The data rate is 1 Mbps on either bus — using both does NOT double it. And 1553 allows only one active Bus Controller at a time.

RS-232 & Serial.clue: On an ARINC 429 bus, the 'HI' differential voltage that represents a binary '1' sits near this level.
RS-232 & Serial.explanation: ARINC 429 uses approximately +10 V differential for HI (binary 1), −10 V differential for LO (binary 0), and 0 V for the null between bits. The +3.3 V and +5 V levels are common in logic-family signaling (CMOS/TTL), not ARINC 429. +24 V is typical of power distribution, not databus signaling.

RS-232 & Serial.clue: This is a key characteristic of RS-232 signals.
choices: Differential signaling at ±10 V
Single-ended signaling referenced to ground at
Current-loop signaling at 20 mA
Optical pulses at infrared wavelengths
RS-232 & Serial.explanation: RS-232 is single-ended: each signal is referenced to a common ground, with voltages typically in the ±3 V to ±15 V range. Differential signaling at ±10 V describes ARINC 429. Current-loop at 20 mA is a different legacy signaling scheme. RS-232 is electrical, not optical.

RS-232 & Serial.clue: This is the typical wiring configuration used by RS-485 in aircraft systems.
choices: A differential twisted pair shared by up to 32 transmitters and receivers in a multi-drop bus
A single-ended wire referenced to ground
A fiber-optic ring with regenerative repeaters
Three separate wires for transmit, receive, and ground with no termination
RS-232 & Serial.explanation: RS-485 uses a differential twisted pair on a multi-drop bus supporting up to 32 drivers and 32 receivers. Single-ended wiring is RS-232. RS-485 is electrical, not optical. Termination resistors at each end of the bus are standard to prevent reflections.

RS-232 & Serial.clue: This is the key difference between RS-422 and RS-485.
choices: RS-422 uses optical fiber while RS-485 uses copper
RS-422 supports one driver and up to 10 receivers
RS-422 is single-ended while RS-485 is differential
RS-422 runs at 10 Mbps while RS-485 is limited to 100 bps
RS-232 & Serial.explanation: RS-422 is one-talker / many-listener; RS-485 allows many talkers and listeners to share the same bus. Both are differential (neither is single-ended). Both use copper. Both support a range of data rates — the distinction is in driver count, not peak rate.

RS-232 & Serial.clue: This limitation of RS-232 makes it impossible to simply tap a third device onto an existing GPS-to-FMS serial link.
choices: The need for a bus controller that RS-232 does not include
A required 120-ohm terminator that most RS-232 devices lack
RS-232 is point-to-point, supporting only two devices per link
Insufficient data rate to carry GPS data at the aircraft's update rate
RS-232 & Serial.explanation: RS-232 is strictly point-to-point — one link connects exactly two devices. Tapping a third device on the same pair does not work. To add a third you need a second RS-232 link, a multi-drop standard like RS-485, or a gateway that fans out. RS-232 has no bus controller, no 120-ohm terminator, and more than enough speed for GPS data.

Digital Fundamentals.clue: This is a key advantage of digital databuses like ARINC 429 over traditional analog signal wiring.
choices: Ability to multiplex many data values on one wire pair with built-in error
Higher voltages that are less susceptible to lightning strikes
Elimination of the need for receivers in avionics units
Reduced need for any shielding on cable runs
Digital Fundamentals.explanation: Multiplexing (many data values per wire pair) and built-in error checking (parity bit) are core advantages. Digital signals are NOT higher voltage than typical aircraft levels, receivers are still required, and shielding remains important to minimize EMI.

Digital Fundamentals.clue: This is the number system essential to digital avionics data processing.
choices: Decimal (base 10)
Binary (base 2)
Octal (base 8)
Hexadecimal (base 16)
Digital Fundamentals.explanation: All digital electronics ultimately store and process binary (base-2) data — each bit is a 0 or 1. Octal and hexadecimal are convenient human shorthand (base 8 and base 16 respectively) that map to groups of bits, but the underlying processing is always binary.

Digital Fundamentals.clue: In the context of avionics data buses, this is what 'word length' refers to.
choices: The physical length of the cable required for one bit
The time in milliseconds that one word takes to transmit
The number of words a bus can carry per second
The total number of bits that make up a single transmitted word
Digital Fundamentals.explanation: Word length is the total bit count of a single word — 32 bits for ARINC 429, 20 bits for MIL-STD-1553. Cable length, transmission time, and throughput are related but distinct concepts.

Digital Fundamentals.clue: In avionics, this acronym stands for 'Built-In Test Equipment' — a self-monitoring feature that reports a unit's health.
choices: BITS
BIST
BITE
BIT
Digital Fundamentals.explanation: BITE stands for Built-In Test Equipment. BITS is not a standard avionics acronym. BIST (Built-In Self-Test) is a related semiconductor-design term. BIT (Built-In Test) is the test itself; BITE is the equipment that performs the test.

Digital Fundamentals.clue: This is what the parity bit in an ARINC 429 or MIL-STD-1553 word lets the receiver do.
choices: Synchronize the receiver's clock to the transmitter's bit rate
Identify which transmitter on the bus originated the word
Measure the data rate the transmitter is currently using
Detect whether a bit was corrupted during transmission
Digital Fundamentals.explanation: Parity is a single-bit error-detection technique. ARINC 429 uses ODD parity — the parity bit is set so the total number of '1' bits in the 32-bit word is odd. If the receiver counts an EVEN number of 1's, at least one bit flipped and the word is dropped. Parity does NOT synchronize clocks (Manchester encoding does that for 1553), does NOT identify sources (the Label/RT address does), and does NOT measure rates.

Bus Troubleshooting.clue: This is a common cause of intermittent communication loss on an ARINC 429 bus.
choices: The receiving unit is powered off
The transmitter is configured for the wrong data rate
A lightning strike in the previous flight
Chafed cable, loose pins, or a broken shield ground
Bus Troubleshooting.explanation: Physical wiring issues — chafing, loose pins, broken shield grounds — are the top causes of intermittent ARINC 429 loss. A powered-off receiver would cause continuous (not intermittent) loss, as would a data-rate mismatch. A lightning strike would typically produce persistent damage, not intermittent symptoms.

Bus Troubleshooting.clue: This is the appropriate first step when ARINC 429 data errors are observed in a system test.
choices: Verify physical-layer integrity
Replace the transmitting unit
Reconfigure all receivers to a different data rate
Update the aircraft flight manual
Bus Troubleshooting.explanation: Physical-layer faults cause most bus errors. Always start with cable continuity, connectors, and shield grounding. Replacing units or reconfiguring receivers without evidence of their involvement wastes time and can introduce new problems.

Bus Troubleshooting.clue: This is the primary purpose of a digital databus analyzer in avionics troubleshooting.
choices: To supply electrical power to the bus during testing
To physically repair damaged wiring
To calibrate the aircraft's altimeter
To capture and decode bus traffic so technicians can verify data
Bus Troubleshooting.explanation: A databus analyzer captures traffic and decodes it into readable words (labels, data fields, parity status, etc.). It does not supply power, perform physical repairs, or calibrate analog instruments — those are different tools.

Bus Troubleshooting.clue: This is a primary purpose of using a digital databus analyzer in a general-aviation avionics shop.
choices: To clean oxidation from connector pins
To physically re-terminate cables
To verify and troubleshoot data exchange between integrated
To perform altimeter static-pressure leak checks
Bus Troubleshooting.explanation: A GA shop uses the databus analyzer to verify correct data exchange between integrated avionics and to isolate intermittent problems that cannot be resolved with a multimeter or oscilloscope alone. The other options describe unrelated maintenance tasks.

Bus Troubleshooting.clue: A 1553 bus that worked for months begins showing intermittent bit errors after an avionics-bay inspection. A bus analyzer shows 'ringing' on the waveform. This is the most likely cause.
choices: A 78-ohm terminator has come loose or open-circuited
The Bus Controller software version is out of date
The Remote Terminals are drawing excessive bus current
The 1 Mbps data rate has drifted outside spec
Bus Troubleshooting.explanation: MIL-STD-1553 requires a 78-ohm terminator at each end of the main cable. A missing or damaged terminator causes the signal to reflect off the end and collide with new transmissions — exactly the 'ringing' seen on an analyzer. Onset right after maintenance points to a terminator that was disturbed. BC software, RT current draw, and data-rate drift produce different symptoms, not ringing.

final.clue: An ARINC 429 data word is exactly 32 bits long. Which bit acts as the parity bit for error checking?
choices: Bit 1
Bit 8
Bit 32
There is no parity bit
final.explanation: ARINC 429 words are 32 bits long. Bit 32 is exclusively used as an Odd Parity bit—set to 1 or 0 to ensure the total number of 1s in the word is an odd number, allowing basic error detection.

final.clue: MIL-STD-1553 uses a bi-directional, half-duplex architecture controlled by a Bus Controller. The devices responding to the controller are known as what?
choices: Slaves
Remote Terminal
Transceivers
Sub-systems
final.explanation: In a 1553 bus, the central controller is the Bus Controller (BC), and it commands up to 31 Remote Terminals (RTs) that act upon those commands.

final.clue: The AFDX (Avionics Full-Duplex Switched Ethernet) standard, used extensively on modern aircraft like the A380 and 787, is defined by which ARINC specification?
choices: ARINC 429
ARINC 717
ARINC 664
ARINC 818
final.explanation: ARINC 664 (specifically Part 7) defines AFDX, which brings deterministic Ethernet networking to avionics, vastly reducing wiring weight compared to point-to-point ARINC 429.
